text: Hi, I am Dr. Arun Prasad, Senior Surgeon at Indraprastha Polo Hospitals. I do robotic surgery for bariatrics, GI and thoracic. Let me show you how the XI robot feels like. So firstly, I will show you the backside of the robot where we have the controls.
The surgeon stands behind the robotic system, gripping the clutches similar to an airport trolley, demonstrating how pressing them allows the robot to move forward, backward, or turn to either side.

The robotic surgical system begins the docking process for upper abdominal surgery, moving forward and turning to the patient's right for lateral docking. The robot and instruments are positioned and ready for the procedure.

Further, I can move this to various sides. For example, I can move this backward or i can move this forward i can move this to this side i can move it to this side so this is the various side movement and when the surgery finishes i can go back to the original so this is the deploying of the thing when the surgery is finished then we go on to store which means we go back to the original position.

The robotic system is seen moving back to its original position. The surgeon demonstrates the connection of a typical ATMM trocar with a robotic instrument, which is inserted similarly to a laparoscopic instrument. The instrument is capable of rotating 360 degrees within the trocar.

The robotic fingers demonstrate their ability to move in multiple angles, unlike a human finger. A sterile drape is applied, and the trocar is docked by pressing it into place. The endoscope is installed for targeting, and the robot provides audible instructions. An instrument is inserted into the trocar.

The robotic arm is positioned and adjusted downward to its desired location. The surgeon removes an instrument by pressing a release mechanism, then inserts a new instrument, which is recognized by the system as indicated by a flashing green light. Once the light turns blue, the instrument is ready for use.

The robotic instrument returns to its original position, showcasing its ability to move in various angles with nine degrees of freedom. The surgeon demonstrates the controls that allow the instrument to be maneuvered in multiple directions, highlighting the advantage for complex dissections. The grip on the instrument enables the surgeon to move the robot around with precision.

So, these are some of the benefits of the robot which we have just seen. Next, I will take you to the console. Now that we have seen the parts of the robot, I will show you how the console works. So, this is the robotic console where the surgeon sits and now there is a screen here which tells me how to log in. So I now look for my name and touch my name. So this takes it back to the position where I had set it. So it's like you know Mercedes where you set your own position.

After that you can still control things by these controls which are here. Now this moves the, this moves this handle down, up. This moves this view box up and down. It also can tilt the view box to my choice. And finally I have the foot controls. So now this foot tray comes out, left foot controls, switching of instrument, camera, and clutch. Right foot will control coterie and coagulation of the right instrument, coterie coagulation of the left instrument. If I have got the stapler inside, then depending upon which side I have, it will also control the clamping and cutting of the staplers.

Finally, we come to the finger controls. So I put this finger control and I am looking inside while doing the surgery and this movement you can see, this is a wrist movement. unlike finger movements or instruments so this entire wrist movements are what are translated inside the abdomen or the chest as we will start seeing in the video of the surgical procedure and there i will show you how these movements are taking place inside the abdomen
The robotic arms and instruments inside the abdomen move in response to the surgeon's hand movements at the console, demonstrating the translation of external controls to internal surgical actions.
